coredata.dc:title: Modification of carbon dots derived from biomass by exogenous nitrogen doping: Action mechanism and difference analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yinghui
Yong, Chao
Tong, Bihai
Li, Yu
Wang, Nan
Lei, Ying
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots derived from biomass (B-CDs) is a burgeoning nanomaterial with great application potential, attracting increasing attention for its sustainability, economy, and eco-friendliness. However, most of B-CDs had low quantum yields and emitted blue fluorescence, limiting their application. In this work, B-CDs were modified through exogenous nitrogen doping in the microwaved-assisted hydrothermal process. Considering the complex composition of biomass, we selected the “cellulosic” and “nitrogen-rich” biomass as representative raw materials and two reagents with different nitrogen-containing structures as nitrogen sources. Various combinations of nitrogen sources and biomass produced different modification effects by regulating the chemical potential of C and N atoms. These differences were reflected in nitrogen-doped contents and states, and sp2 C structure, resulting in different luminescent properties. It tells suitable nitrogen dopants should be selected for different biomass. The nitrogen-doped contents were up to 21.49%. Dopamine played a role in the formation of quaternary N. Melamine facilitated the formation of pyridinic N and amine N. A higher proportion of nitrogen-doped, pyridinic N, amine N, and sp2 C could reduce the bandgap, thus improving quantum yields and generating longer wave emissions. The absolute quantum yields enhanced from 2.95% to 35.39%. Green fluorescence and a 732 nm long-wave emission appeared in the modified B-CDs. A slight increase in temperature was conducive to the optimization of luminescence. The fluorescence lifetimes of 6.17–33.20 ns were obtained due to the combination of radiative and non-radiative recombination in different proportions. The B-CDs showed good fluorescence stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass
Carbon dots
Exogenous nitrogen doping
Microwave-assisted hydrothermal
Optical property
Modification mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped B-CDs by MHTC method
Physicochemical characteristics of the nitrogen-doped B-CDs
Optical properties of the nitrogen-doped B-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number 51974005 ]; the Collaborative Innovation Project of Universities in Anhui Province [grant number GXXT-2020-007 ], and the Scientific Research Foundation of Anhui University of Technology .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process of the nitrogen doped B-CDs.
FTIR spectra of the (a) CSdh-CDs, (b) CSm-CDs and (c) Sdh-CDs.
(a) XPS full-scan survey spectra, (b) high-resolution XPS of C1s, (c) high-resolution XPS of N1s, and (d) high-resolution XPS of O1s of the CSdh-CDs, CSm-CDs, and Sdh-CDs.
Schematic diagram of the chemical structure of nitrogen sources and B-CDs.

Images of (a) CSdh-CDs in supernatants, (b) CSdh-CDs in supernatants under UV light; (c) CSm-CDs in supernatants, (d) CSm-CDs in supernatants under UV light; (e) Sdh-180-CDs in supernatants, (f) Sdh-180-CDs in supernatants under UV light; (g) Sdh-200-CDs in supernatants, (h) Sdh-200-CDs in supernatants under UV light.
UV–Vis spectra of nitrogen-doped modified CDs.
PL emission spectra of (a) CSdh-CDs, (b) CSm-CDs, (c) Sdh-180-CDs, (d) Sdh-200-CDs.
Time-resolved photoluminescence spectra of the (a) CSdh –CDs, CSm –CDs, (b) Sdh-180-CDs, Sdh-200-CDs.
Drawings made with nitrogen-doped B-CDs as fluorescent ink.
Percentage of nitrogen and its chemical states determined using N1s high-resolution XP spectra and sp2 C determined using C1s high-resolution XP spectra in CSdh-CDs, CSm-CDs, and Sdh-CDs.
Multiexponential fitting for time-resolved fluorescence dynamics of the nitrogen-doped CDs.

head.ce:title: Modification of carbon dots derived from biomass by exogenous nitrogen doping: Action mechanism and difference analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Anhui University of Technology, Ma'anshan, 243032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Civil Engineering and Architechitecture, Anhui University of Technology, Ma’anshan 243032, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Metallurgy Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Ma'anshan, 243032, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Metallurgy Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Ma’anshan 243032, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots derived from biomass (B-CDs) is a burgeoning nanomaterial with great application potential, attracting increasing attention for its sustainability, economy, and eco-friendliness. However, most of B-CDs had low quantum yields and emitted blue fluorescence, limiting their application. In this work, B-CDs were modified through exogenous nitrogen doping in the microwaved-assisted hydrothermal process. Considering the complex composition of biomass, we selected the “cellulosic” and “nitrogen-rich” biomass as representative raw materials and two reagents with different nitrogen-containing structures as nitrogen sources. Various combinations of nitrogen sources and biomass produced different modification effects by regulating the chemical potential of C and N atoms. These differences were reflected in nitrogen-doped contents and states, and sp2 C structure, resulting in different luminescent properties. It tells suitable nitrogen dopants should be selected for different biomass. The nitrogen-doped contents were up to 21.49%. Dopamine played a role in the formation of quaternary N. Melamine facilitated the formation of pyridinic N and amine N. A higher proportion of nitrogen-doped, pyridinic N, amine N, and sp2 C could reduce the bandgap, thus improving quantum yields and generating longer wave emissions. The absolute quantum yields enhanced from 2.95% to 35.39%. Green fluorescence and a 732 nm long-wave emission appeared in the modified B-CDs. A slight increase in temperature was conducive to the optimization of luminescence. The fluorescence lifetimes of 6.17–33.20 ns were obtained due to the combination of radiative and non-radiative recombination in different proportions. The B-CDs showed good fluorescence stability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have a wide range of potential applications in various fields, including sensors, bioimaging, catalysis, theranostics, and light-emitting diodes for their remarkable multicolor fluorescence, robust chemical inertness, aqueous solubility, and biocompatibility [1–3]. In particular, the green biomass-based carbon dots (B-CDs) prepared with biomass as the precursor have more advantages in low toxicity, sustainability, environmental friendliness, economy, etc. However, at present, chemical reagents are mainly used as precursors for synthesizing CDs. The existing synthesis methods, such as arc discharge, electrochemical, and combustion approach, have the problem of secondary pollution. Therefore, it is a significant work to take biomass or its waste as green carbon sources and explore a green and efficient way to convert them into CDs, attracting more and more attention in the academic community. The microwave-assisted hydrothermal (MHTC) treatment is a green, economical, and facile thermochemical conversion method. Many researchers have used the method to dispose of and convert biomass. However, most researchers focused on the research and application of biological carbon in solid-phase products. The aqueous phase products were less concerned because of their high COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand) content, which was difficult to utilize directly for environmental pollution risk. Klemmer et al. reused aqueous phase products to improve the carbon yield, but there was no apparent increase after reuse [4]. Moreover, most carbon products could only be used as the precursors of high-quality carbon materials, resulting in a low direct utilization rate.

In the previous study of MHTC conversion of different biomass, we discovered a kind of approximate spherical nanoparticles with a diameter of less than 10 nm in the aqueous phase. The test results showed a new type of fluorescent CDs with good photoluminescence (PL) properties [5], which proved the feasibility of preparing B-CDs from biomass by the MHTC method. Some researchers had also prepared B-CDs using respectively silk fibroin, goose feather, bamboo tar, mangosteen pulp, pulp-free lemon juice, and Hylocereus undatus as carbon sources by the MHTC method [6–9].

However, the research on B-CDs was still in the primary stage. Most of the B-CDs emitted fluorescence only in a blue region and had low quantum yields (QYs) of less than 10% [10,11], which could not meet the requirements of industrial production and application. For these problems, heteroatom doping could be an effective means of regulation. Many researchers adopted it to modify the CDs derived from chemical reagents. Different heteroatom doping had different effects on the properties of CDs. Even if the same atom was doped, different doping locations, binding types, and doping amounts led to various absorption and emission characteristics [12].

Some studies indicated that the N-doping inhibited the oxygen defect state in CDs, thus improving the QYs [13]. The doping of P and S atoms formed substitution defects and generated new bandgaps, thus changing the electronic and luminescent properties of CDs [14,15]. B-doping activated electron transitions, thus enhancing fluorescence [16]. And F-doping improved the fluorescence stability of CDs when pH changed [17]. In particular, there were more studies on N-doping. Ethylenediamine, melamine, and ammonia solution were used as the nitrogen sources for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) [7,18]. Xie et al. [19] used ethylenediamine as a nitrogen source to obtain N-CDs with a quantum yield of 14.4%. Yang et al. [20] performed hydrothermal treatment on different amino acids to form additional bandgaps, getting N-CDs with the highest quantum yield of 41.3%. Miao et al. [21] used citric acid and urea to synthesize N-CDs with multicolor emission through controlling surface oxidation degree. These multicolor N-CDs were separated by silica column chromatography.

Most reports about doping modification focused on the CDs derived from chemical reagents, but there were few studies on the optimization of B-CDs. In our previous research, B-CDs derived from “cellulosic” and “nitrogen-rich” biomass through the MHTC method were studied, respectively [5]. The results showed that B-CDs derived from nitrogen-rich biomass had the best luminescent performance and the highest quantum yield, demonstrating that nitrogen doping was also beneficial to improving the QYs for B-CDs. Biomass raw materials contain heteroatoms such as N and S, which are suitable for preparing heteroatom-doped CDs. However, the self-doping amount of endogenous nitrogen in biomass obtained in the synthesis of B-CDs was only 2.3%–7.2%, which improved the absolute QYs of B-CDs by 2.09%–3.98% at most, only to a small extent [5]. It is necessary to explore a method to enhance the nitrogen doping degree and modification effect for B-CDs. Moreover, due to the complex composition of biomass materials, it is more challenging to analyze the luminescence mechanism and regulate the optical properties of B-CDs compared with CDs synthesized from chemical reagents. Therefore, exploring the doping regulation mechanism, improving QYs, and optimizing luminescence properties will promote the practical application of B-CDs. Not only will the risk of environmental pollution caused by aqueous phase products be reduced, but also the competitiveness of conversion technology for biomass will be further improved.

Given the above, dopamine hydrochloride and melamine were used as exogenous nitrogen sources to improve the QYs and optimize the luminescence performance of B-CDs. Because of the variety and complexity of biomass, the selection of biomass raw materials should be representative. Based on our earlier research, cellulose and protein are the critical components that distinguish and affect the structure and properties of B-CDs. Hence, the representative cellulosic biomass cotton stalk (contains 48.7% cellulose) and nitrogen-rich biomass soymeal (contains more than 40% crude protein) were selected as raw materials to prepare B-CDs by the MHTC method. Both of the two biomass chosen are massive productive biomass wastes. The two nitrogen sources have different nitrogenous structures. In this work, we explored the nitrogen doping effect and the differences in doping of the two nitrogen sources. The correlation between the chemical structure and the luminescence properties of nitrogen-doped B-CDs was analyzed. Based on that, the regulation mechanism of B-CDs doped by different nitrogen sources was discussed. The reaction temperature is a crucial factor affecting the MHTC synthesis of B-CDs significantly [22]. Hence the effect of reaction temperature on the modification of nitrogen doping was preliminarily investigated.

Soymeal and cotton stalk were obtained from rural areas in Jiangsu, China. The biomass materials were dried for 8 h at 105 °C and then crushed by a pulverizer (DLF-18). The crushed powder was sifted uniformly with a 60 mesh sifter to reduce the influence of particle size on the synthesis of B-CDs. Melamine and dopamine hydrochloride were purchased from Nanjing Wanqing Chemical Co., Ltd. The Smart-Q DI water purification system provided Deionized (DI) water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped B-CDs by MHTC method
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped B-CDs were prepared via the MHTC method at the reaction temperatures of 180 and 200 °C, respectively, maintaining residence time at 2 h. The experiment steps were briefly illustrated in Fig. 1 . When the nitrogen additive was dopamine hydrochloride, the raw material was cotton stalk, and the reaction condition was 180 ℃, 2 h, the process was described as follows. First, 2 g of cotton stalk powder and 2 g of dopamine hydrochloride were thoroughly mixed with 50 ml of DI water to enhance interaction with the microwave and transferred to a Teflon™-lined reaction vessel. The vessel was sealed and placed in a microwave workstation (XO-SM100, Nanjing Xianou, China). Then the reactants were heated to 180 °C with magnetic stirring under microwave power of 300W and maintained for 2 h. After the reaction, the vessel was cooled to room temperature, and the products were collected. The supernatant in the products was separated from the large carbon particles by ultracentrifuging at 10000 rpm for 5 min. The centrifugation process was repeated twice for better separation. Next, the supernatant containing B-CDs was dialyzed against DI water for 10–20 h through a dialysis membrane with a 1000 Da molecular weight cutoff. Part of the resulting sample was saved in the solution state, and another part was freeze-dried by a lyophilizer for subsequent characterization. The nitrogen-doped B-CDs synthesized from different biomass materials and nitrogen sources were denoted as follows: CSdh-CDs (cotton stalk and dopamine hydrochloride), CSm-CDs (cotton stalk and melamine), Sdh-180-CDs and Sdh-200-CDs (soymeal and dopamine hydrochloride; the number of the subscript represented the reaction temperatures of 180 and 200 °C, respectively).

Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were measured through a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer to analyze the surface functionalization of the samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was determined using a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI electron spectrometer with an Al Kα excitation source. The morphology images of the B-CDs were recorded using a JEM-2100F HRTEM electron microscope. The XRD patterns were collected by a Miniflex 600 X-ray powder diffraction. Raman test was performed by a confocal Raman spectrometer (Labram HR800). UV–Vis absorption spectra were taken on a TU-1901 spectrometer at room temperature. PL measurements were carried out at various excitation wavelengths using a PerkinElmer LS55 spectrometer. The absolute PL QYs were obtained using an FLS980 steady/transient state fluorescence spectrometer by a photomultiplier tube sphere as the Eml detector's light source and a xenon XEL lamp as the light source. The lifetimes were characterized using an FLS980 steady/transient state fluorescence spectrometer through time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC). Zeta potential was determined by the nanoparticle size and Zeta potential analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS ZEN3600).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical characteristics of the nitrogen-doped B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the B-CDs had been determined as approximate spherical nanoparticles by TEM tests in our previous study (Fig. S1). The broad diﬀractions presented in XRD patterns and the low graphitization degree obtained by Raman tests demonstrated the amorphous structure with poor crystallinity for B-CDs (Figs. S2 and S3) [5]. The functional characteristics of the nitrogen-doped B-CDs were analyzed through FTIR spectra, as shown in Fig. 2 .

These nitrogen-doped B-CDs mainly had the functional groups of –OH (3351–3467 cm−1), –NH (3133–3224 cm−1), –NH of amide II band (1502–1554 cm−1), C–N (1286 cm−1) and C=C/C=O (1436–1610 cm−1), suggesting the formation of nitrogen-doped states and conjugated sp2 C structure of carbon core state [7,23–25]. However, there were apparent differences in peak positions and intensity of the functional groups in the three B-CDs. Unlike the other two B-CDs, the –NH stretching vibration of prime amide in CSm-CDs was stronger and had double absorption bands, which might be attributed to the combined effect of intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonds. The sharp peak of C–N in CSdh-CDs had the strongest intensity. The functional characteristics of Sdh-CDs had similarities to that of S-CDs (Fig. S4) (same materials soymeal, without nitrogen dopant) and CSdh-CDs. That is, the structures of biomass and nitrogen source both affected the surface structure of B-CDs. Note that compared with S-CDs, the stretching vibration of –OH in Sdh-CDs was weaker, while the nitrogen-doped structures were stronger, which confirmed that more abundant nitrogen-containing functional groups were obtained by introducing exogenous nitrogen sources than by biomass self-doping. In addition, the –NH and C–N characteristic peaks of Sdh-200-CDs were slightly stronger than that of Sdh-180-CDs, suggesting that a slight increase in temperature might be conducive to improving nitrogen doping degree.

The chemical structure and element states of the nitrogen-doped B-CDs (prepared under the reaction condition of 180 ℃, 2 h) were further analyzed by XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. 3 . As could be seen from the XPS full-scan spectra (Fig. 3[a]), the modified B-CDs all contained carbon (C1s, 284.9 eV), nitrogen (N1s, 398.9 eV), and oxygen (O1s, 530.9 eV) [26]. However, different combinations of nitrogen sources and biomass resulted in significant differences in the nitrogen doping amount, 8.56% in CSdh-CDs, 21.49% in CSm-CDs, and 10.86% in Sdh-CDs. Based on our previous research, the nitrogen doping content in CS-CDs (cotton stalks as biomass materials) and S-CDs without any nitrogen source was only 2.3% and 7.2%. Obviously, adding exogenous nitrogen sources increased the proportion of nitrogen in reactants, promoting the migration of nitrogen from reactants to B-CDs, thus significantly improving the degree of nitrogen doping in the B-CDs. Compared with biomass self-doping, the nitrogen content in CSm-CDs increased the most. Through the difference analysis of raw materials, it was found that the higher nitrogen content was in nitrogen source and the less in biomass, the more significant improvement of the nitrogen doping proportion in B-CDs would be.

Different nitrogen sources also affected the element states of B-CDs. The N1s (Fig. 3[c]), C1s (Fig. 3[b]), and O1s (Fig. 3[d]) spectra were fitted for further analysis. The N1s spectra were deconvoluted to four peaks at 398.5, 399.7, 400.2, and 401.3 eV, which were attributed to pyridinic N, amine N, pyrrolic N, and quaternary N, respectively [18]. The C1s spectra showed five peaks at 284.5, 284.8, 286, 287.3, and 288.1 eV, corresponding to sp2 C, sp3 C, C–N, C–O, and C=O, respectively. The spectra of O1s presented two peaks at 531.2 (C=O) and 532.6 eV (C–OH/C–O–C) [27].

The N1s spectra showed distinct characteristics, with bimodal (CSdh-CDs and Sdh-CDs) and unimodal (CSm-CDs) characteristics, respectively. The proportions of four nitrogen-doped states formed in the three modified B-CDs were different, as shown in Table 1 . Combining with Fig. 4 , the relationship between the nitrogen source structure and the nitrogen-doped states was analyzed. The quaternary N with the highest content appeared in CSdh-CDs, obtained from dopamine hydrochloride as the nitrogen source. It was worth noting that dopamine possessed the structural characteristics for carbon skeleton formation. Among the four nitrogen states in Sdh-CDs, the proportion of quaternary N was also the highest, which suggested that dopamine hydrochloride played a role in the formation of quaternary N. And quaternary N had a positive effect on the anti-interference of CDs [28]. The proportion of –NH2 functional group in melamine is significantly higher than that in dopamine hydrochloride, which might be the essential reason for the highest amine N content in CSm-CDs. Besides amine N, the content of pyridinic N in CSm-CDs was also the highest among all the B-CDs, further indicating that the chemical structure of melamine had an impact on the formation of the binding states of amine N and pyridinic N. Bu et al. studied the effect of nitrogen doping concentration on the construction of pyridinic N and quaternary N. They found that a higher N-doping concentration would improve the doping probability of pyridinic N, in comparison, the formation of quaternary N could realize at a lower N-doping concentration [29]. This conclusion is consistent with the results obtained in this paper: CSm-CDs had the highest concentration of N-doping and got the highest proportion of pyridinic N, while CSdh-CDs and Sdh-CDs had low N-doping concentration, resulting in a high ratio of quaternary N, and the lowest N-doping concentration corresponded to the highest ratio of quaternary N (CSdh-CDs). The doping regulation results could be ascribed to the fact that different nitrogen sources could regulate the chemical potential of C and N atoms, thus achieving the regulation of N-doping concentration and type.

Among the three modified B-CDs, the nitrogen-doped content of Sdh-CDs was in the middle, but the content of sp2 C was the highest (Table 1). The sp2 C dominated the intrinsic state luminescence. The different nitrogen-doped content, the relative proportions of varying nitrogen-doped states, and the sp2 C structure would have different effects on the luminescence characteristics of the modified B-CDs, as analyzed in detail in the following section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the nitrogen-doped B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The supernatants and luminescence of the modified B-CDs are shown in Fig. 5 . In order to investigate the anti-agglomeration ability of B-CDs in the supernatants, the samples were refrigerated for a long time. The supernatants of CSdh-CDs and Sdh-CDs were dark brown and emitted cyan closer to green fluorescence under UV light. By comparison, the CSm-CDs supernatant was brown and emitted cyan closer to blue fluorescence. From the color of supernatants, the carbonization degree and concentration of CSdh-CDs and Sdh-CDs were higher [30]. The original supernatants (1), supernatants diluted to sixfold (2), tenfold volumes (3), and colorless (4), and their luminescence were compared. The original supernatants showed an inner filter effect of fluorescence. The inner filter effect in CSm-CDs supernatant was weakest, and the luminescence was strongest when the supernatant was diluted to tenfold volume. CSdh-CDs luminescence was relatively weak. When Sdh-CDs supernatant was diluted to colorless, it emitted bright cyan fluorescence, exceeding the fluorescence brightness of CSm-CDs, which differed from CSdh-CDs. In the case of the same nitrogen source, there were big gaps in PL ability between CSdh-CDs and Sdh-CDs, which might be related to the agglomeration among CSdh-CDs particles. Based on our previous studies, CS-CDs derived from cotton stalk tended to agglomerate. The agglomeration could cause fluorescence quenching through resonance energy transfer or π-π interaction [31]. However, the S-CDs derived from soymeal had a better anti-agglomeration performance, which might be one of the reasons why Sdh-CDs came from behind and far surpassed CSdh-CDs in luminescence. To optimize the luminescence of CSdh-CDs, the newly prepared sample was purified by high-speed centrifugation for several times, and its concentration was adjusted to reduce the inner filter effect. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching caused by agglomeration was alleviated by ultrasonic dispersion, so that CSdh-CDs presented better green luminescence, as shown in Fig. 7[a].

The stability of the above supernatants was characterized. The zeta potential was Sdh-180-CDs (35.5 mV), Sdh-200-CDs (28.5 mV), CSdh-CDs (26.5 mV), CSm-CDs (−19.0 mV) from high to low. The higher the zeta potential was, the better the stability was [32]. The B-CDs modified by dopamine hydrochloride as a nitrogen source had better stability, which is consistent with the above analysis. The supernatants of Sdh-CDs still had good fluorescence after being refrigerated for a long time, exhibiting a better anti-agglomeration performance. Furthermore, the zeta potential decreased with increasing temperature.

The absorption properties of the nitrogen-doped B-CDs were studied through UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 6
. The absorption characteristics of Cdh-CDs were observed at the wavelengths of 270 and 299 nm, assigned to the π–π* transition probably dominated by the conjugated sp2 C of the carbon core. Besides the two absorption peaks similar to those of Cdh-CDs, Sdh-CDs had an extra absorption peak at 366 nm, attributed to the n–π* transition that corresponded to the effect from some nitrogen-doped states [33,34]. The bandgaps corresponding to the main absorption peaks of B-CDs were calculated: 3.92 eV (299 nm), 3.35 eV (336 nm), 2.94 eV (366 nm, 180 °C) and 2.91 eV (366 nm, 200 °C), respectively (the method and figures [Fig. S5] of fitting calculation were shown in the supporting information). In Sdh-CDs, a higher proportion of nitrogen-doped, pyridinic N, amine N, and sp2 C could reduce the bandgap [22,35], thus generating absorption in the longer wavelength. The bandgap values from 3.92 to 2.91 eV further confirmed this conclusion. CSdh-CDs were doped with the highest proportion of quaternary N related to the anti-interference of CDs, and correspondingly the lowest proportion of the other nitrogen states related to luminescent properties, which was an important reason for the absence of absorption peak at 366 nm and weak luminescence. The above comparative analysis indicated the difference in doping interaction between different raw materials and the same nitrogen source.

The absorption peaks of CSm-CDs were located about 299 and 336 nm. Compared with CSdh-CDs, CSm-CDs had higher ratios of pyridinic N and amine N. Pyridinic N contributed a delocalized electron to the π system between carbon atoms, narrowing the bandgap between π and π*, thus increasing the probability of electron excitation at the same excitation energy [36,37]. Therefore, an absorption peak at the longer wavelength of 336 nm (bandgap of 3.35 eV) appeared. It was further verified that different nitrogen sources could modify the absorption characteristics of B-CDs by regulating nitrogen-doped structure.

Fig. 7 shows the fluorescence emission spectra of the nitrogen-doped B-CDs. The two nitrogen sources also caused differences in emission characteristics. The CSm-CDs displayed a single peak. In contrast, The CSdh-CDs and Sdh-CDs displayed double emission peaks, which suggested two types of dominant luminescence centers. The two luminescence centers represented by the emission peaks were denoted as I and II. For CSdh-CDs, the emission intensity of the luminescence center II was stronger than that of center I at the excitation wavelength of 280–340 nm, and center II was the dominant luminescence center. Its maximum PL intensity was obtained as the excitation wavelength was 340 nm, and the optimal emission wavelength was 502 nm at the green light wave band. Hence the PL of the CSdh-CDs was greenish. The emission spectra corresponding to the excitation wavelength of 280–340 nm almost presented an excitation-independent emission character, suggesting that the luminescence structure that could be excited was relatively constant, without change in quantity or type. As the absorption spectra indicated, the π–π* transition of intrinsic conjugated structure dominated the luminescence when the excitation wavelength was less than 340 nm. This is a relatively simple and constant luminescent structure. However, as the excitation wavelength increased from 360 nm, the emission intensity of center I was enhanced to dominate the luminescence. At this point, the dominated luminescence was the n–π* transition of surface states based on its absorption characteristics. These emission peaks were redshifted for the change of the surface states that could be excited by the longer waves.

CSm-CDs presented an excitation-dependent emission character. The optimal emission was obtained at 436 nm as the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. It's worth noting that there's also an emission peak at 732 nm, demonstrating that nitrogen-doped B-CDs have the potential of long-wave emission. Longwave emission is the key to many practical applications of B-CDs, such as bioimaging, theranostics and photoelectrocatalysis, but it is also one of the current research difficulties. However, the peak intensity at 732 nm was not strong enough, so we further optimized B-CDs to enhance the generation of this luminescent structure based on this modification idea, and have obtained red fluorescence.

The emission spectra of Sdh-CDs were similar to that of CSdh-CDs. The dominant luminescence center of Sdh-200-CDs showed a transformation from II to I at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Its optimal emission was obtained at 472 nm, close to the wavelength of green light, so the fluorescence of Sdh-200-CDs was greenish-blue. With the increase of temperature, the optimal excitation wavelength was redshifted from 360 to 400 nm, and the optimal emission wavelength was redshifted from 442 to 472 nm. This is because a slight increase in temperature improved the nitrogen doping degree, thus making the excitation energy decrease (the bandgap reduced from 2.94 to 2.91 eV) and the emission characteristics move towards the longer wavelength.

The absolute QYs of these nitrogen-doped B-CDs were measured as: 11.22%, 28.42%, 30.53%, and 35.39% for CSdh-CDs, CSm-CDs, Sdh-180-CDs, and Sdh-200-CDs, respectively. The doped nitrogen could suppress the oxygen defect states in CDs, thus improving the QYs significantly compared with the B-CDs (2.95% for CS-CDs and 4.92% for S-CDs) without adding a nitrogen source. However, CSm-CDs with the highest nitrogen content did not get the highest quantum yield, which indicated that the improvement of QYs was related not only to the doped nitrogen content but also to the other specific chemical structure. The highest quantum yield was obtained by Sdh-180-CDs with a higher sp2 C content (23.95%) besides the difference in proportions of doped nitrogen states. That is, the conjugated sp2 C might also be one of the reasons for the increase of QYs. Interestingly, the gap of QYs between CSdh-CDs and Sdh-CDs was big when the nitrogen source was the same. This result showed the differences in the adaptation degree of different biomass and nitrogen dopants. Obviously, in terms of optimizing luminescent structures and properties, the adaptation degree between soymeal and dopamine hydrochloride was better. The quantum yield of Sdh-200-CDs was higher than that of Sdh-180-CDs, which further confirmed that a slight increase in temperature was conducive to a gain of the nitrogen doping degree and thus improving the quantum yield.

Fig. 8 shows the time-resolved PL spectra of these nitrogen-doped B-CDs, which were fitted with two fluorescent lifetime components. The fitting results are listed in Table 2 . The two-lifetime components of CSm-CDs were less different, indicating simple or single PL origin, both attributed to radiative recombination luminescence. Therefore, the luminescence effect of CSm-CDs was better and its average lifetime was shorter. However, there were wide variations in the two-lifetime components of B-CDs doped with dopamine hydrochloride. Taking the case of CSdh-CDs, its shorter lifetime of 1.6 ns was due to radiative recombination, accounting for 24%, while its longer lifetime of 32.50 ns was ascribed to non-radiative recombination, accounting for 76%. CSdh-CDs had a high concentration and were easy to polymerize. Excessive concentration [38] and polymerization of CDs [39] would cause non-radiative cross relaxation, resulting in a longer fluorescence lifetime and weaker PL intensity. So the time-resolved PL spectrum of CSdh-CDs decayed faster. According to the PL effect of gradually diluted CSdh-CDs supernatants (Fig. 5 [b]), the concentration reduction did not make the luminescence significantly stronger. And B-CDs prepared from cotton stalk were less stable and easier to agglomerate based on our previous studies. Hence, the non-radiative recombination of CSdh-CDs was most likely related to its polymerization. Unlike CSdh-CDs, the PL properties of Sdh-CDs came from behind to improve considerably with gradual dilution (Fig. 5 [f], [h]). These results meant that the problem of weak fluorescence for non-radiative recombination caused by concentration could be solved by dilution. And the zeta potential results showed good anti-aggregation performance of the Sdh-CDs supernatant. In consequence, excessive concentration was an essential reason for the non-radiative recombination of Sdh-CDs.

In addition, non-radiative recombination might also be caused by the non-radioactive decay of the carbon nuclear state, non-radiative recombination of electron hole pairs on oxygen-containing functional groups (such as –COOH and –OH, etc.), and the inhibition of intrinsic luminescence [35,40,41]. The complex surface states in B-CDs underwent complicated de-excitation pathways, resulting in various PL lifetimes. The proportion of the longer lifetime for Sdh-CDs increased as the temperature rose from 180 to 200 °C, and thus the average lifetime increased. Sdh-200-CDs obtained the longest fluorescence life of 33.20 ns, much longer than 19.50 ns reported by Zheng et al. at that time [42]. It is necessary to explore further the decay dynamics mechanisms and the corresponding application of B-CDs in combination with the characteristics of a large proportion of non-radiation recombination and long fluorescence lifetime.

Finally, the supernatants of the nitrogen-doped B-CDs were used as fluorescent ink, and their fluorescent stability was tested. The fluorescent ink was used to draw patterns on paper without background fluorescence and irradiated with strong UV light for a long time, as shown in Fig. 9 . It was found that these B-CDs had bright color rendering fluorescence, and no apparent fluorescence quenching phenomenon occurred, indicating their good fluorescence stability against photobleaching.

In this work, carbon dots derived from biomass were modified with dopamine hydrochloride and melamine as exogenous nitrogen sources during the MHTC process. Given the variety and complexity of biomass, cotton stalk and soymeal were selected as the representative cellulosic and nitrogen-rich biomass raw materials, respectively. Various combinations of nitrogen sources and biomass produced differences in the nitrogen-doping amount, the ratios of nitrogen-doped states, and conjugate sp2 C, leading to the differences in optical properties of B-CDs. The difference analysis showed that suitable nitrogen dopants should be selected for different biomass. Compared with the B-CDs without adding nitrogen sources, the absolute QYs were significantly improved from 2.95% to 28.4% and from 4.92% to 35.39%, respectively. The absorption and emission characteristics of the modified B-CDs were both redshifted. CSdh-CDs showed green luminescence, and CSm-CDs presented a 732 nm long-wave emission. Sdh-CDs had the highest QYs and better anti-agglomeration performance. The longest fluorescence life of 33.20 ns was obtained. The nitrogen-doped B-CDs showed good color rendering ability and fluorescence stability. The results and mechanism analysis provided methodological support for the precise regulation of biomass conversion into CDs. Based on this idea, we have preliminarily obtained red-light B-CDs. This work can enhance its application potential and improve the competitiveness of disposal and high value-added product conversion technology of biomass waste.

Yinghui Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Chao Yong: Investigation, Validation. Bihai Tong: Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Yu Li: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Nan Wang: Investigation. Ying Lei: Investigation, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number 51974005]; the Collaborative Innovation Project of Universities in Anhui Province [grant number GXXT-2020-007], and the Scientific Research Foundation of Anhui University of Technology.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2022.113144.
